workflows.name: Debugging Loop
workflows.purpose: Turns a single 'fix this error' message into a structured, iterative root-cause loop instead of shallow one-off patches.
workflows.whenToUse: You've been stuck on a bug for more than one exchange, the same error keeps reappearing after a 'fix', or you want the actual root cause instead of a band-aid.
workflows.template:
You are an expert {{LANGUAGE}} debugger. I'm working on {{PROJECT_CONTEXT}}.

ERROR:
{{ERROR_MESSAGE}}

RELEVANT CODE:
{{CODE_SNIPPET}}

WHAT I'VE ALREADY TRIED:
{{ATTEMPTS}}

Do the following, in order, and show your reasoning at each step:
1. State your top 3 hypotheses for the root cause, ranked by likelihood.
2. For hypothesis #1, identify the exact line(s) or logic responsible.
3. Propose a fix and explain why it addresses the root cause, not just the symptom.
4. Give me one concrete test to run that confirms the fix worked.
5. If the fix doesn't work, tell me exactly what to check next (hypothesis #2).

Stop and wait for my actual test result before proposing another change.

variables.label: Language / framework
variables.default: Python

variables.label: Project context
variables.default: a FastAPI backend for a RAG pipeline

variables.label: Full error / stack trace
variables.default: paste the full traceback here

variables.label: Relevant code
variables.default: paste the function or file section here

variables.label: What you've already tried
variables.default: e.g. checked types, added logging, restarted kernel

examples: Filled with a KeyError in a vector-store lookup: the model correctly identified a race condition between an async cache write and read, instead of just suggesting a try/except.
bestPractices: Paste the FULL traceback, not just the last line — the root cause is often several frames up.
Report the exact new error after testing a fix, instead of 'still broken' — that's what keeps the loop tight.
Mention anything that changed recently (dependency update, refactor) since regressions usually trace back there.
workflows.expectedOutput: A ranked list of hypotheses, a targeted fix tied to the top hypothesis, and one verification test — ready to feed the result of back into the next iteration.

workflows.name: Code Review & Refactor Assistant
workflows.purpose: Replaces a generic 'does this look okay?' with a structured, multi-angle review covering correctness, edge cases, performance, security and readability.
workflows.whenToUse: Before a commit/PR, after finishing a feature, or when code 'works' but you're not confident it's solid.
workflows.template:
Act as a senior {{LANGUAGE}} engineer doing a thorough code review.

CODE TO REVIEW:
{{CODE_SNIPPET}}

CONTEXT: {{CONTEXT}}
PRIORITIES (ranked): {{PRIORITIES}}

Review across these dimensions, and only flag genuine issues (skip a section if there's nothing meaningful to say):
1. Correctness — logic errors, off-by-one issues, incorrect assumptions.
2. Edge cases — inputs or states that would break this.
3. Performance — real bottlenecks, not micro-optimizations.
4. Security — injection, unsafe deserialization, secrets, auth gaps.
5. Readability & structure — naming, duplication, function size.

For each issue: quote the exact line, explain the risk in one sentence, then give the corrected code.
End with a short refactored version of the full snippet if changes are non-trivial.

variables.label: Language
variables.default: TypeScript

variables.label: Code to review
variables.default: paste code here

variables.label: What this code does / where it runs
variables.default: an Express API route handling file uploads

variables.label: What matters most here
variables.default: security, then readability, then performance

examples: Given an unauthenticated file-upload handler, the model flagged a missing MIME-type check as the top security issue before touching style.
bestPractices: State your priority order — a review of a hackathon prototype and a production auth service should weigh differently.
Include the surrounding context (framework, who calls this) so suggestions aren't generic.
Ask it to skip sections with nothing to say — this avoids review reports padded with non-issues.
workflows.expectedOutput: A structured review report with quoted lines, risk explanations, corrected snippets, and an optional full refactor.

workflows.name: System / Architecture Design Assistant
workflows.purpose: Turns a vague project idea into a concrete architecture with explicit tradeoffs, instead of a single opinionated answer.
workflows.whenToUse: Starting a new project, scaling an existing one, or choosing between competing technical approaches.

variables.label: Project description
variables.default: an AI agent platform that runs scheduled research tasks and emails summaries

variables.label: Key requirements
variables.default: multi-user, needs to run tasks reliably even if the app restarts

variables.label: Scale / constraints
variables.default: solo developer, small budget, expecting under 1000 users initially

variables.label: Your experience level
variables.default: comfortable with Python/Node, new to distributed systems

examples: For a solo-dev agent platform, the model recommended a simple job queue over a full message broker, explicitly trading scalability for time-to-ship — with a clear note on when to revisit that call.
bestPractices: State your real constraints (team size, budget, timeline) — architecture 'best practice' without constraints is generic advice.
Ask it to be opinionated, not just list options — a menu of tradeoffs without a recommendation just shifts the decision back to you.
Revisit this workflow at each major milestone; architecture decisions age.
workflows.expectedOutput: A recommended architecture with named components, a clear data flow, two compared alternatives, failure points, and a concrete scaling trigger.

workflows.name: README / Project Documentation Generator
workflows.purpose: Generates a genuinely useful README from a rough project description, instead of a generic template with placeholder text.
workflows.whenToUse: Wrapping up a project for GitHub, your portfolio, or before handing it off / submitting it.

variables.label: Project name
variables.default: AgentFlow

variables.label: One-line description
variables.default: A lightweight framework for building and chaining autonomous AI agents

variables.label: Tech stack
variables.default: Python, LangChain, FastAPI, Redis

variables.label: Key features
variables.default: visual agent chaining, retry logic, cost tracking per run

variables.label: Rough setup notes
variables.default: clone repo, pip install -r requirements.txt, set OPENAI_API_KEY, run uvicorn main:app

variables.label: Who's reading this
variables.default: other developers evaluating whether to use this in their own project

examples: Given rough setup notes, the model produced numbered, copy-pasteable install steps and inferred a sensible 'Usage' example from the described features.
bestPractices: Give real setup notes, even messy ones — the model structures them, it doesn't need them pre-formatted.
Specify your audience — a README for recruiters reads differently than one for contributors.
Regenerate this whenever features change significantly rather than hand-patching an old README.
workflows.expectedOutput: A complete, ready-to-paste README.md with real setup commands and a usage example, not placeholder text.

workflows.name: Research Paper Summarizer
workflows.purpose: Extracts the actual substance of a paper — contribution, method, limitations — instead of a surface-level abstract restatement.
workflows.whenToUse: Doing a literature review, deciding whether a paper is relevant to your research, or prepping for a discussion/presentation.
workflows.template:
Read the following paper content and analyze it critically.

PAPER CONTENT / ABSTRACT:
{{PAPER_TEXT}}

MY RESEARCH AREA: {{RESEARCH_AREA}}

Provide:
1. Plain-language summary (3-4 sentences, no jargon).
2. The single core contribution — what's actually new here.
3. Methodology, briefly, and one genuine critique or limitation of it.
4. Stated limitations vs. limitations you notice that the authors didn't mention.
5. How this connects to my research area: {{RESEARCH_AREA}} — is it directly relevant, tangential, or a useful contrast?
6. Three follow-up questions I should investigate next.

Be honest if the contribution is incremental — don't oversell a paper's novelty.

variables.label: Paper text / abstract
variables.default: paste abstract or key sections here

variables.label: Your research area
variables.default: retrieval-augmented generation for domain-specific QA systems

examples: Given an abstract on prompt compression, the model separated the authors' stated limitation (dataset size) from an unstated one it identified (no evaluation on multi-turn dialogue).
bestPractices: Paste as much of the actual paper as you can, not just the abstract — methodology critique needs the methods section.
Always state your research area — 'relevance' is meaningless without a reference point.
Ask it to be honest about incremental work; this avoids treating every paper as groundbreaking.
workflows.expectedOutput: A structured critical summary: contribution, methodology critique, relevance to your work, and concrete follow-up questions.

workflows.name: New Concept / Tech Explainer
workflows.purpose: Explains an unfamiliar concept calibrated to your actual background, with a check for understanding — instead of a generic Wikipedia-style answer.
workflows.whenToUse: Encountering a new framework, algorithm, or term while building, and you want to actually understand it, not just copy-paste around it.
workflows.template:
Explain {{CONCEPT}} to me.

MY BACKGROUND: {{BACKGROUND}}
WHY I'M LEARNING THIS: {{GOAL}}

Structure the explanation as:
1. One-sentence definition, in plain language.
2. An analogy grounded in something from my background above.
3. A minimal, runnable code example (if applicable) with inline comments explaining each part.
4. The most common misconception beginners have about this.
5. When to use this vs. the main alternative(s), and why.

Then quiz me with 2 short questions to check my understanding — don't give the answers yet, wait for my responses.

variables.label: Concept / technology
variables.default: vector embeddings and cosine similarity

variables.label: Your background
variables.default: MCA student, comfortable with Python and basic ML, new to NLP internals

variables.label: Why you're learning this
variables.default: building a semantic search feature for a project

examples: For a student with a Python/ML background, the model anchored the embeddings analogy in coordinate geometry the student already knew, instead of a generic 'like GPS coordinates' analogy.
bestPractices: Be specific about your background — 'beginner' vs 'comfortable with X, new to Y' changes the whole explanation.
State why you're learning it — the explanation should serve your actual goal, not be exhaustive.
Actually answer the quiz questions instead of skipping them; that's what turns this into retained knowledge.
workflows.expectedOutput: A calibrated explanation with an analogy, a minimal code example, common pitfalls, and two comprehension-check questions.

workflows.name: Autonomous Agent Prompt Builder
workflows.purpose: Defines a complete, guardrailed system prompt for an autonomous AI agent instead of an ad-hoc instruction that drifts over time.
workflows.whenToUse: Building an agent that needs to operate over multiple steps with tools, and needs consistent behavior and clear boundaries.
workflows.template:
You are {{AGENT_ROLE}}, an autonomous agent. Your goal: {{GOAL}}.

AVAILABLE TOOLS: {{TOOLS}}

OPERATING LOOP:
1. Assess the current state against the goal.
2. Decide the single next action using only the tools listed above.
3. Execute and observe the result.
4. Re-assess: is the goal met, partially met, or blocked?
5. Repeat until the goal is met or a stop condition below is triggered.

STOP CONDITIONS: {{STOP_CONDITIONS}}
GUARDRAILS (never do these): {{GUARDRAILS}}

OUTPUT FORMAT: For every step, output exactly:
THOUGHT: <your reasoning>
ACTION: <tool name + input>
OBSERVATION: <result, or "awaiting execution">

If you are blocked or a guardrail would be violated, stop and output BLOCKED: <reason> instead of taking an action.

variables.label: Agent role/persona
variables.default: a research assistant agent

variables.label: Agent's goal
variables.default: find and summarize the 5 most relevant recent papers on a given topic

variables.label: Tools available
variables.default: web_search(query), fetch_page(url), save_summary(text)

variables.label: Stop conditions
variables.default: 5 relevant papers found and summarized, OR 10 search attempts reached with no new relevant results

variables.label: Guardrails / never do
variables.default: never fabricate a citation; never fetch pages outside the search results

examples: A research agent with a clear stop condition (5 papers OR 10 attempts) avoided an infinite refinement loop that a goal-only prompt had previously fallen into.
bestPractices: Always pair a positive stop condition (goal met) with a negative one (attempt limit) — this is what prevents runaway loops.
List tools exactly as they're callable, including parameter names, so the agent's ACTION lines are directly executable.
Keep guardrails as concrete forbidden actions, not vague values like 'be safe'.
workflows.expectedOutput: A complete agent system prompt with an explicit operating loop, stop conditions, guardrails, and a structured Thought/Action/Observation output format.

workflows.name: Project Idea → Spec Generator
workflows.purpose: Converts a rough project idea into a structured spec with MVP scope and milestones, instead of an unstructured brain dump.
workflows.whenToUse: At the very start of a new side project, hackathon, or when an idea needs to become an actionable plan.
workflows.template:
Turn this rough idea into a structured project spec.

ROUGH IDEA: {{IDEA}}
TIME AVAILABLE: {{TIME_AVAILABLE}}
MY SKILLS: {{SKILLS}}

Produce:
1. Problem statement — what problem this solves, in one paragraph.
2. Target user — who feels this problem most acutely.
3. Core features — split into "MVP (must have)" and "Later (nice to have)", with reasoning for the split.
4. Recommended tech stack, matched to my listed skills and the time available.
5. Milestones — a numbered build order (not a calendar), each with a clear "done" definition.
6. Top 3 risks that could derail this, and one mitigation each.

Be realistic about scope given the time available — cut features rather than compress timelines.

variables.label: Rough idea
variables.default: an app that turns messy class notes into spaced-repetition flashcards automatically

variables.label: Time available
variables.default: 3 weekends

variables.label: Your current skills
variables.default: Python, basic React, some experience with LLM APIs

examples: Given a 3-weekend timeline, the model moved multi-user support to 'Later' and defined the MVP as single-user + one input format, with a clear milestone-based build order.
bestPractices: Give a real time budget, not 'whenever I get to it' — vague time inputs produce vague scoping.
List actual current skills, not aspirational ones — the stack recommendation should match what you can ship with now.
Ask it to cut features over compressing timelines; that's the instruction that actually protects a realistic MVP.
workflows.expectedOutput: A scoped spec: problem statement, MVP vs later feature split, matched tech stack, ordered milestones, and named risks with mitigations.

workflows.name: AI Debugging Assistant
workflows.purpose: Analyze technical errors, identify root causes, and provide step-by-step solutions.
workflows.whenToUse: Whenever you're stuck on an error and want a structured, root-cause-first fix instead of a guess.
workflows.template:
[ROLE]
Act as a senior software engineer and debugging expert.

[OBJECTIVE]
Analyze the provided error and provide the best solution.

[CONTEXT]
Programming language: {{LANGUAGE}}
Framework: {{FRAMEWORK}}
Error message: {{ERROR_MESSAGE}}
Previous attempts: {{ATTEMPTS}}

[CONSTRAINTS]
Do not provide random fixes. Explain the root cause first.

[REASONING STRATEGY]
Analyze symptoms, identify possible causes, rank solutions, and recommend the safest fix.

[OUTPUT FORMAT]
1. Error summary
2. Root cause
3. Solution steps
4. Prevention tips

[QUALITY CHECKS]
Verify the solution is technically correct before responding.

variables.label: Programming language
variables.default: Dart / Flutter

variables.label: Framework
variables.default: Flutter (Android build)

variables.label: Error message
variables.default: I am getting a Flutter Gradle build error related to Android dependencies.

variables.label: Previous attempts
variables.default: Cleaned build, invalidated caches

examples: Flutter Gradle build error related to Android dependencies.
bestPractices: Paste the full error message, not a summary.
Mention what you've already tried.
workflows.expectedOutput: Error summary — a concise restatement of the Gradle/Android dependency error. Root cause — the specific dependency conflict or version mismatch responsible. Solution steps — ordered, concrete steps to resolve it. Prevention tips — how to avoid this class of error in future builds.
